dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My water broke suddenly, and I'm experiencing contractions every ten minutes. I think I might be in labor.
Are you alert and aware of your surroundings?
Yes, I know where I am and what time it is.
She is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event with a GCS of 15.
She shows signs of moderate distress due to pain but appears cooperative and responsive.
The pain is at a 10 out of 10 with each contraction.
Vital signs: Blood pressure is 125/84 mmHg, pulse is 93, respirations are 18 per minute, and SpO2 is 97%.
Glucose cannot be obtained at this time, and we do not have a reason for concern based on initial vitals.
My water broke suddenly with a gush of fluid. I've had contractions every 10 minutes lasting about 1.5 minutes. I'm G4P3A0, 37 weeks pregnant, with a due date of September 18th.
Airway is clear and patent, breathing is spontaneous and not labored.
Chest appears symmetric. HEENT is normal with no abnormalities reported. The head is normocephalic.
Abdomen is round, firm, and non-tender. No signs of crowning are present, and perineal exam does not raise concerns.
Skin is pink, warm, and dry with normal turgor. No rashes are reported, and no wounds are observed on the extremities.
Per our field impression, the patient is in active labor with significant pain. We'll proceed with the OB/GYN childbirth/labor/delivery protocol.
I'm starting a 4-lead ECG as per standing orders.
Beginning a 20-gauge IV in the left antecubital vein as per protocol now.
Let's check your vitals again. Blood pressure is 124/89 mmHg, pulse is 77, and SpO2 is 98%.
EKG remains in normal sinus rhythm with no changes observed.
Transport destination is VCU Medical Center as requested by the patient. She has been ambulated to the stretcher and secured for transport.
Upon arrival, the patient was registered at VCU Medical Center and moved to Labor and Delivery Exam Room A. Care was transferred to a registered nurse.
All strapped up and ready for transport. We are now ready to move you to the hospital.
Can you open your eyes on command?
Yes, I can.
Examine eyes open to command, consistent with responsiveness.
Can you tell me your name and date of birth?
I'm Jennifer, born on March 10, 1994.
She is oriented.
Can you squeeze my finger on command?
Yes.
She responded appropriately.
Let me quickly assess your airway. Airway is clear and patent. No signs of obstruction.
Airway is expected to remain patent without immediate intervention.
Breathing is spontaneous and in normal pattern.
Respirations are 18 per minute. Breathing is symmetrical with no signs of overt distress.
No cyanosis or flaring nostrils observed. Pulox reading is within acceptable range.
Let me check for peripheral pulses and cap refill. Pulses are full and regular. Cap refill is less than 2 seconds.
Skin is pink and dry. No signs of perfusion compromise observed.
Blood pressure is 125/84 mmHg. Pulse is 93 beats per minute, strong and regular.
Thank you for sharing. Your symptoms started about 30 minutes ago with water breaking and contractions beginning every 10 minutes.
Have you had any pre-existing conditions or complications in this pregnancy?
No complications. I've had all required prenatal care.
I see from your chart that you have a known allergy to penicillin.
Any other known allergies?
No others, that I can recall.
Currently, you are not on any medications, correct?
Correct.
Can you give us a quick summary of your pregnancy history?
I'm G4P3A0. This is my fourth pregnancy, third live birth, no previous miscarriages.
When was the last time you had anything to eat or drink?
Earlier tonight before bedtime, but I'm not exactly sure.
What happened to prompt your 911 call?
I woke up to a sudden gush of fluid, then contractions began pretty soon after.
Skin appears dry and with normal turgor. No areas of concern identified at this time.
Examining head and neck. No signs of injury or abnormality noted.
No discharge or obstruction found.
No signs of oral injury or loss of function.
Chest is symmetric. No signs of trauma or pain.
II: Chest auscultated, no adventitious sounds.
Assessing abdomen, it is firm but without overt signs of distress.
All extremities are intact. No signs of injury or instability in the back.
Perineum shows no external signs of blood or fluid.
Performing the EKG as part of our routine OB assessment. This will check for any rhythm changes.
EKG results show no acute changes. Rhythm is stable.
Based on our findings, we continue following Protocol 6-2 for childbirth and labor.
No medications administered at this time with current clinical presentation.
IV access and EKG are our current interventions.
Repeating vitals for tracking. Blood pressure is now 124/89 with a pulse of 77, and SpO2 is 98%.
IV is well placed, running free, and EKG is in place with no acute changes.
Still feeling pain at a 10 out of 10?
Yes, each contraction is painful and lasts roughly 1.5 minutes.
Are the contractions still consistent with a frequency of about 10 minutes?
Exactly, they haven't improved or changed in pattern.
When was your last contraction?
A few moments ago, maybe about 30 seconds.
No new developments in your condition or symptoms since calling?
Nothing major. Just ongoing pain and contractions.
VCU Medical Center remains our destination for your care.
Movement to stretcher is secure.
You are now safely loaded for transport. Please remain with us during the transfer.
Everyone is ready for movement now.
Patient is ready for the hospital, vitals and interventions have been reviewed.
We have flagged the receiving hospital and given a full report to ensure readiness.
She was safely transferred onto a hospital bed in Labor and Delivery Exam Room A.
RN has confirmed stable condition and is preparing for further management.
